title: Adam Azim's biggest test: Jack Catterall and the step into championship water
summary: Adam Azim is 23, unbeaten and finishing everyone in front of him. Jack Catterall is exactly the kind of seasoned southpaw who tends to slow that sort of hurry down.
body:
Adam Azim has rarely had to find out what the later rounds feel like. Fifteen fights, fifteen wins, twelve by knockout, and his last five have all ended early. At 23 he has the look of a fighter the sport is in a hurry to crown. Jack Catterall is the opponent who tends to put a stop to that kind of momentum.

The unbeaten 23-year-old

Azim's profile is power and pace. He carries an 80 per cent knockout rate and a five-fight run of stoppages, the form of a young man walking through the level beneath him. He is the taller fighter here too, 5ft 11in against Catterall's 5ft 7in, and he works from an orthodox stance. What the record cannot yet show is how he behaves when an opponent is still there in the championship rounds.

The man sent to ask the questions

Catterall, 32, brings 33-2 over a far longer career and an opposite way of winning. Nineteen of his victories have come on the cards, his knockout rate sits at 42 per cent, and his southpaw style has carried him through hard, deep fights and back from a disputed defeat. He has lived in the rounds Azim has mostly skipped.

Youth and power against the miles

The contrasts are clean. Nine years, four inches of height, and a stance switch from orthodox to southpaw that complicates the range for both men. Azim has to prove his finishing carries into deeper water against someone comfortable there. Catterall, at 32, has to make a 23-year-old work long enough to find that out. Whoever imposes his preferred pace tends to win this kind of fight.
